title: Surface lone-pair polarization probed by quantum-geometric transport in tellurium
authors: Nathanael N. Batista, Wendel S. Paz, Manuel Suárez-Rodríguez, Pierpaolo Fontana, Victor Velasco, Marcus V. O. Moutinho, Chang Niu, Peide D. Ye, Marco Gobbi, Fèlix Casanova, Luis E. Hueso, Caio Lewenkopf, Marcello B. Silva Neto
abstract: Stereochemically active lone pairs are ubiquitous microscopic sources of polarity in molecules and solids, but their collective behavior in crystals is often hidden by symmetry or confined to surfaces. Here we show that quantum-geometry transport provides a sensitive probe of surface lone-pair polarization in trigonal tellurium. This surface polarization appears microscopically as an inversion-odd dipolar component of the crystal potential, which shifts the center of mass of Bloch wavepackets and produces quantum-geometric corrections to their velocity. We describe this lone-pair polar texture through a minimal three-component lattice model, and we show that the resulting linear and nonlinear transport coefficients probe, respectively, the second and first moments of the net polarization field. Because rectified voltages in tellurium flakes are directly proportional to the surface lone-pair polarization, our results provide a microscopic route to understanding and engineering polarization-driven, quantum-geometric electronic devices based on tellurium allotropes.
summary: This work establishes that surface lone-pair polarization in materials like tellurium can be probed using quantum-geometric transport measurements. By analyzing nonlinear electrical responses, the authors successfully map measurable quantities to the first and second moments of the surface polarization field. This provides a powerful, microscopic route for engineering polarization-driven electronic devices.
main_problem: The paper aims to develop a microscopic understanding of how surface lone-pair polarization in crystals, like tellurium, influences electronic transport properties.
main_result: Quantum-geometric transport theory successfully links measurable nonlinear electrical responses (second and third harmonics) to the first and second moments of the surface polarization field.
method: The authors combine first-principles calculations (DFT) with quantum-geometry transport theory, deriving effective Hamiltonians and analyzing nonlinear current expansions.
model_or_system: The system is trigonal tellurium, where the surface lone-pair polarization is modeled as an inversion-odd dipolar component of the crystal potential, leading to quantum-geometric corrections to Bloch wavepacket dynamics.
key_observables: Linear and nonlinear transport coefficients ($\delta\sigma_{	ext{geo}}^{xx}, \chi_{xxx}$), DC rectification voltage ($V_{	ext{DC}}$), and temperature/thickness scaling of harmonic responses.
important_parameters: Surface lone-pair polarization field ($E_0$), temperature ($T$), flake thickness ($d$), and gate voltage ($\mu$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on perturbative expansions in electric fields and current densities, and the use of a coarse-grained lattice model for polarization thermodynamics.
figures_summary: Figures show experimental data confirming angular dependencies ($\sin^2	heta, \sin^3	heta$) for harmonic responses, and theoretical plots mapping polarization moments ($\langle P_x angle_T, \langle P_x^2 angle_T$) against temperature or thickness.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical origin of surface polarization, deriving the quantum-geometric transport formalism, linking transport coefficients to polarization moments, and finally demonstrating the predictive power of this framework using harmonic response measurements.
why_interesting: The methodology provides a concrete, measurable link between a subtle, symmetry-breaking microscopic property (lone-pair polarization) and macroscopic, nonlinear electronic transport phenomena, which is valuable for designing quantum-geometric devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Quantum-geometric transport. Experimental data from Ref. [15]. (a) An AC current Iω is injected while the sample is rotated in the xz plane by an angle θ with respect to the crystallographic axes. (b) First-harmonic longitudi- nal resistance V ω ∥/Iω = Rω ∥measured as a function of θ. The linear response is proportional to −δσgeo xx (E2 0) sin2 θ, with δσgeo xx < 0 for any E0. (c) and (d) Second-harmonic V 2ω ∥ /(Iω)2

Figure 3. Excess electronic density (red) and charge de- pletion (blue) relative to the atomic densities of constituent atoms. The three panels correspond to slices at (a) z = 0, (b) z = Lz/3, and (c) z = 2Lz/3, for the three non-equivalent atoms in the unit cell. The black arrows indicate the local inversion-odd dipolar slopes computed from the gradient of the Hartree potential, providing the directions of the local lone-pair dipoles.

Figure 2. Inversion-odd wavepacket shift. Schematic il- lustration of the one-dimensional crystal potential, V (r) = Veven(r)+Vodd(r). The inversion-even component Veven(r) de- fines symmetric wells centered at the ionic positions r0 (brown circles). The inversion-odd component Vodd(r) introduces an asymmetric local slope −eE0 ≡∇Vodd|rc≃r0, which shifts the total potential minimum away from the centrosymmetric ref- erence (dashed line) by an offset ∆(solid line). This inversion- breaking crystal field causes a positional shift in the center of mass of the localized Bloch wavepacket.

Figure 5. Lone-pair polarization. (a) Schematic showing how the loss of coordination at the boundaries breaks the bulk C3 symmetry, generating a finite polarization near the surfaces. Here, E0 < 0 (and thus χxxx(E0) > 0) produce the angular dependence shown in Fig. 1(d). (b) Measured ρ3 D|χxxx(d)| as a function of flake thickness d for samples S1, S2, S3, and S4 [40], demonstrating a clear 1/d dependence. The deviation of S1 from this scaling is discussed in the main text.

Figure 4. Gate-voltage dependence. Experimental data from Ref. [40] showing scaling behavior of the harmonic re- sponses as a function of the applied back-gate voltage Vbg for selected samples (labeled on the y-axis). (a) First-harmonic response, V ω ∥/Iω ∼µ−5/2, and (b) second-harmonic response, V 2ω ⊥/(Iω)2 ∼µ−7/2, displaying the scaling laws predicted from the QG correction to δσgeo xx and χxxx, respectively.
